metadata.credit: Data and analysis provided by Draftsim
metadata.author: Draftsim

set_overview.summary: Final Fantasy is a high-synergy set with well-balanced archetypes. Success requires drafting coherent, synergistic decks rather than relying on bombs or 5-color piles. The format mostly plays as a 2-color synergy environment, with GUx (Towns/Ramp) being the main splash-friendly archetype.

color_notes.{U} Blue: Strong tempo and noncreature-spell synergies. Anchors UR Big Spells (tier 1) and contributes to WU Artifacts/Skies and UB Control. Deep removal/tempo tools (Ice Magic, Thunder Magic, Syncopate).
color_notes.{G} Green: Ramp, fatties, and the Towns/Ramp engine. Best fixing access via Travel the Overworld. Anchors BG Graveyard (tier 1).
color_notes.{B} Black: Deep removal pool plus graveyard/sacrifice synergies. Cloud of Darkness is the single best signpost uncommon in the set.
color_notes.{W} White: Solid creatures and equipment, especially in RW and WU. Job Select equipment plays a starring role.
color_notes.{R} Red: The worst color in the set. Limited wizard support for BR, and RG Landfall is weakly supported. Still has efficient burn and Choco-Comet/Thunder Magic for spell decks.

key_gameplay_notes: Format is mostly 2-color; only GUx splashes more thanks to dual-land synergies.
Synergy beats bombs — pure goodstuff piles underperform coherent archetypes.
Unconditional removal (Sephiroth's Intervention, Fate of the Sun-Cryst) is worth splashing.
Job Select equipment is generally solid and fits most decks.
Tiered spells (Thunder Magic, Ice Magic, Fire Magic) reward casting at the highest tier; pair with UR Big Spells payoffs.

mechanics.name: Legendaries
mechanics.summary: The set has many legendary creatures but limited "legends matter" payoffs. Venat, Heart of Hydaelyn is an excellent rate due to how easy it is to trigger.
mechanics.performance: Legendaries are pushed individually rather than as a tribal theme.

key_examples.name: Venat, Heart of Hydaelyn
key_examples.type_line: Legendary Creature — Elder Wizard // Legendary Creature — God

mechanics.name: Job Select
mechanics.summary: Equipment-style cards offering dual functionality: enter as an equipment or use the alternate mode. Job Select cards are generally pretty solid and slot into most decks.
mechanics.performance: Reliable role-players across all archetypes; standouts in RW and WU.

mechanics.name: Tiered Spells
mechanics.summary: Three-stage spells that trigger multiple payoffs depending on the cost paid. Thunder Magic enables 4+ payoffs in UR Big Spells.
mechanics.performance: Format-defining for UR; secondary in BR (Fire Magic) and UR/UB (Ice Magic).

mechanics.name: Saga Creatures (Summons)
mechanics.summary: Multi-chapter saga creatures representing iconic Final Fantasy summons. Four-chapter examples include G.F. Ifrit and Fat Chocobo; three-chapter examples include Primal Garuda.
mechanics.performance: Top uncommons (Summon: Fenrir, Summon: Shiva) are format-defining. Several commons are playable.

key_examples.name: Summon: Fenrir
key_examples.note: The best uncommon in the set.

mechanics.name: Transforming DFCs
mechanics.summary: Double-faced cards that transform under specific conditions. Mostly on rares; Crystal Fragments is a representative example.
mechanics.performance: Individually powerful but rare; not a draftable theme.

key_examples.name: Crystal Fragments
key_examples.type_line: Artifact — Equipment // Enchantment Creature — Saga Construct

mechanics.name: Cycling
mechanics.summary: The common cycle shows wide quality variance. Malboro overperformed; Balamb T-Rexaur is pretty good; Ice Flan and Hill Gigas are mediocre but playable; Cloudbound Moogle is probably playable but not good.
mechanics.performance: Useful filler that smooths draws; quality varies card-by-card.

mechanics.name: Adventure Lands
mechanics.summary: Lands with adventure-style flexible modes. Zanarkand, Ancient Metropolis is the only good adventure town.

mechanics.name: Towns
mechanics.summary: Land-based ramp/fixing engine that powers GUx. Travel the Overworld is the truth — the engine of GUx decks. Prishe's Wanderings and Reach the Horizon tutor towns.
mechanics.performance: Defines the GUx archetype; weaker without dedicated tutors.

key_examples.name: Travel the Overworld
key_examples.note: The truth — engine of GUx decks.

tier_1.name: UR Big Spells
tier_1.strategy: Reward casting noncreature spells (especially 4+ mana). Tiered spells like Thunder Magic and Ice Magic enable multiple payoffs in one cast. Combine cheap interaction with payoff creatures that snowball off your spell count.

tier_1.name: BG Graveyard / Midrange
tier_1.strategy: Cloud of Darkness is the single best signpost uncommon in the set. Lean on backbone commons and the deep BG removal + creature pool to grind out games.

key_cards.name: Cloud of Darkness
key_cards.note: The single best signpost uncommon in the set.

tier_2.name: WB Sacrifice / Midrange
tier_2.strategy: Three-tier theme: payoffs (Judge Magister Gabranth, Al Bhed Salvagers), sacrifice outlets (Phantom Train, Namazu Trader, Ahriman), and fodder creatures. Uses Job Select equipment extensively to add value to the sacrifice loop.

tier_2.name: RW Aggro / Equipment
tier_2.strategy: Slower than typical RW, emphasizing incremental advantages via equipment. Voltron potential through Adelbert Steiner. Star lineup of legendary creatures stacks Job Select gear for big swings.

tier_2.name: WU Artifacts / Skies
tier_2.strategy: Tempo-focused deck with fliers and Job Select equipment. Cid, Timeless Artificer is the strong signpost; Tidus, Blitzball Star is the worst multicolor uncommon in the set.

key_cards.name: Tidus, Blitzball Star
key_cards.note: The worst multicolor uncommon in the set.

tier_2.name: GUx Towns / Ramp
tier_2.strategy: 5-color good-stuff archetype enabled by Towns and dual lands. Travel the Overworld and Omega, Heartless Evolution are the primary payoffs. Ignis Scientia provides value and graveyard hate.

tier_3.name: UB Control / Good Stuff
tier_3.strategy: Lightly themed archetype that emphasizes 2-for-1s and kill spells over hard synergy. Locke Cole and Ultimecia, Time Sorceress provide value. Sidequest: Hunt the Mark pairs well.

key_cards.name: Ultimecia, Time Sorceress
key_cards.type_line: Legendary Creature — Human Warlock // Legendary Creature — Nightmare Warlock

tier_3.name: GW Go Wide / Midrange
tier_3.strategy: The go-wide theme felt kind of like a bust. Works better with summons payoffs Garnet, Princess of Alexandria and Rinoa Heartilly than with pure token strategies.

tier_3.name: BR Wizards / Bleeder
tier_3.strategy: Limited wizard support. Cornered by Black Mages is the key payoff. Plays like mono-removal with cards like Fire Magic, edict effects, and Malboro.

tier_3.name: RG Landfall / Stompy
tier_3.strategy: Weakly supported because red is the worst color in the set. Bird tokens from landfall are capped at 2 toughness. Payoffs include Gladiolus Amicitia, Rydia, Summoner of Mist, and Sabotender.

general_tips: Stay 2-color unless you are drafting GUx Towns — dual lands and Travel the Overworld are what enable splashing.
Prioritize coherent synergies over loose bombs; the set rewards drafters who commit to an archetype.
Cloud of Darkness is the single best signpost — take it over almost anything if you are open in BG.
Summon: Fenrir is the best uncommon in the set; first-pick over most rares.
Avoid red unless you are in UR Big Spells; RG and BR are the weakest pairings.
Thunder Magic and Ice Magic enable multiple payoffs in UR Big Spells — pick them highly when in that lane.
